message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  A kinetic approach is developed, in a “tutorial style” to describe the evolution of an epidemic with spread taking place through contact. The “infection - rate” is calculated from the rate at which an infected person approaches an uninfected susceptible individual, i.e. a potential recipient of the disease, up to a distance
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  , where the value of
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  may lie between
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>min</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>≤ p ≤ p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>max</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  . We consider a situation with a total population of
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  individuals, living in an area
                  <jats:italic>A, x(t)</jats:italic>
                  amongst them being infected while
                  <jats:italic>x</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>(t) = β′x(t)</jats:italic>
                  is the number that have died in the course of transmission and evolution of the epidemic. The evolution is developed under the conditions (1) a faction α
                  <jats:italic>(t)</jats:italic>
                  of the
                  <jats:italic>[N-x(t) – x</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>(t)]</jats:italic>
                  uninfected individuals and (2) a
                  <jats:italic>β(t)</jats:italic>
                  fraction of the
                  <jats:italic>x(t)</jats:italic>
                  infected population are quarantined, while the “source events” that spread the infection are considered to occur with frequency
                  <jats:italic>υ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  . The processes of contact and transmission are considered to be Markovian. Transmission is assumed to be inhibited by several processes like the use of “masks”, “hand washing or use of sanitizers” while “physical distancing” is described by
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  . The evolution equation for
                  <jats:italic>x</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) is a Riccati - type differential equation whose coefficients are time-dependent quantities, being determined by an interplay between the above parameters. A formal solution for
                  <jats:italic>x(t)</jats:italic>
                  is presented, for a
                  <jats:italic>“graded lockdown”</jats:italic>
                  with the parameters,
                  <jats:italic>0≤ α(t), β(t)≤1</jats:italic>
                  reaching their respective saturation values in time scales,
                  <jats:italic>τ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>1</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ,
                  <jats:italic>τ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  respectively, from their initial values
                  <jats:italic>α(0)=β(0)=0</jats:italic>
                  . The growth is predicted for several BBMP wards in Bengaluru and in urban centers in Chikkaballapur district, as an illustrative case. Above selections serve as model cases for high, moderate and thin population densities. It is seen that the evolution of
                  <jats:italic>[x(t)/N]</jats:italic>
                  with time depends upon (a) the initial time scale of evolution, (b) the time scale of cure and (c) on the time dependence of the Lockdown function
                  <jats:italic>Q(t) = {[1-α(t)]·[1-β(t)]}</jats:italic>
                  . The formulae are amenable to simple computations and show that in order to curb the spread one must ensure that
                  <jats:italic>Q(∞)</jats:italic>
                  must be below a critical value and the vigilance has to be continued for a long time (at least 100 to 150 days) after the decay starts, to avoid all chances of the infection reappearing.
                </jats:p>
title: Mathematical model study of a pandemic: Graded lockdown approach

reference.unstructured: M.S. Bartlett , An Introduction to Stochastic Processes, Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 1962.
J.N. Kapur , Mathematical Models in Biology and Medicine, Affiliated East West Press, Hyderabad, 1992.
N.T.J. Bailey , The Mathematical Theory of Epidemics, Hafner Publishing Company, New York, 1957.
Kerson Huang , “Statistical Mechanics”, Wiley Eastern, New Delhi, 1975.
M.L. Krasnov , “Ordinary Differential Equations”, Mir Publishers, Moscow, 1987.
Nikita Mehra , “Battling a virus with masks”, The Hindu, May 26, 2020.
M.S. Seshadri & T. Jacob John , “Scripting a new narrative for COVID control”, The Hindu, June, 3, 2020.
Ashish Jha , interview to Narayan Lakshman, “Policy makers must realize that pandemic is still in early stage in India”, The Hindu, July 9, 2020.
Bindu Shajan Perappan , “‘Trace contacts within 72 hours’, States must ensure contacts of 80% of new cases are traced by this time”, The Hindu, July 9, 2020.

“ The second epidemiological report of Corona status in Fars province, from the onset of the epidemic to 9th May, 2020”, Department of Epidemology, Shiraz University of Medical Science, Shiraz, Iran. [in Persian].
